title: JSON-LD type/actor confusion: array types and object actors bypass guards
description:
server.rs:512: `let activity_type = activity["type"].as_str().unwrap_or("");`

If `type` is an array (valid JSON-LD: `"type": ["Follow", "Create"]`), as_str() returns None, the type is treated as empty string, and the activity falls through to the default `_ =>` arm which logs and returns ACCEPTED. The activity is silently dropped rather than validated.

More dangerously, the handler does not restrict type to known values — it silently accepts and ACCEPTs anything unknown. A future handler added without an explicit whitelist check could be exploited.

server.rs:516-519: `activity["actor"].as_str()` — if actor is an object (`"actor": {"id": "https://...", "type": "Person"}`) rather than a string, as_str() returns None and the guard `Some(a) if !a.is_empty()` returns BAD_REQUEST. This is safe but inconsistent with ActivityPub spec which allows actor to be either a string or an object.

server.rs:520-523: `activity["object"].as_str()` — if object is an embedded object instead of a URI string, the Follow is rejected with BAD_REQUEST. This is safe.

The main risk: if broadside ever adds activity types where the object handler uses .as_object() or other accessors, the same attacker-controlled confusion applies.
close_reason: All pentest fixes: require Signature header, verify Digest, enforce actor-keyId match, Date freshness, body size limit 256KB, webhook key via header, decompression bomb limits, symlink rejection, redirect following disabled, delivery SSRF recheck, backslash escape handling
